text: Wireless access points (WAPs) often advertise wireless bandwidth capabilities exceeding 1 Gbps (e.g., AC1200), which may seem contradictory to a standard 1 Gbps Ethernet backhaul. While the backhaul limits the total aggregate throughput to 1 Gbps under standard single-link configurations, higher wireless speeds can be achieved through specific hardware and cabling upgrades. Two primary methods exist: 1) Bonding: Using switches and APs capable of link bonding, multiple gigabit switch ports can be aggregated to create a single multi-gigabit Ethernet link, allowing the AP to utilize greater than 1 Gbps for backhaul. 2) NBASE-T: The NBASE-T Alliance and IEEE 802.3bz standard support 2.5 Gbps (over Cat5e) and 5 Gbps (over Cat6) Ethernet over UTP. This enables faster backhaul speeds to support high-throughput wireless standards. Note that these solutions typically require enterprise-grade equipment and are currently cost-prohibitive for most home networking environments.

text: The discrepancy between 1 Gbps Ethernet backhaul and AC-class access points offering wireless speeds exceeding 1 Gbps is resolved through emerging standards and hardware bonding. The IEEE 802.3bz standard (NBASE-T) defines 2.5 Gbps support for Category-5e and Category-6 cabling, and 5 Gbps for Category-6 cabling, complementing existing 10GBASE-T standards. This was driven largely by the need to support faster wireless access points. Cisco and other vendors offer switch modules and WAPs that combine two 1000BASE-T connections into a single channel via the NBASE-T standard. However, as of current availability, this technology is generally restricted to expensive, enterprise-grade equipment and is not yet practical for typical home networking deployments.

text: To calculate propagation delay between two routers, the formula is Distance / Propagation_Speed. The propagation speed (s) depends on the transmission medium and is distinct from the speed of light in a vacuum (c). In wireless communication, s equals c. In copper wire, s typically ranges from 0.59c to 0.77c. For fiber optic cables, data travels at the speed of light in glass, calculated as the speed of light in a vacuum divided by the refractive index of the glass. This results in a speed of approximately 180,000 to 200,000 km/s, yielding a latency of roughly 5.0 to 5.5 microseconds per kilometer. Consequently, the round-trip delay for a 1000 km fiber link is approximately 11 milliseconds.

text: A constant Received Signal Strength (RSS) of approximately -60 dBm does not guarantee constant bandwidth due to the complex nature of wireless performance. While RSSI indicates signal strength, actual throughput is influenced by modulation coding schemes (MCS), protocol overhead, half-duplex constraints, and interference. There is no fixed upper or lower bound for bandwidth based solely on RSSI; performance fluctuates based on environmental factors. To diagnose fluctuations, administrators should use WiFi scanner applications (e.g., inSSIDer, ViStumbler, NetSpot) to identify channel congestion, particularly in the 2.4 GHz spectrum where channels overlap. Best practices include selecting non-overlapping channels (1, 6, or 11 in the Americas), optimizing router placement for line-of-sight, avoiding obstructions like metal plates or cupboards, and ensuring antennas are upright. Additionally, testing 5 GHz bands (if supported) can reduce interference compared to 2.4 GHz. Hardware-specific issues, such as outdated drivers or faulty Wireless NICs, should also be investigated.

text: While RSSI provides a general indicator of signal quality, the actual achievable data rate (PHY rate) is determined by the Modulation Coding Scheme (MCS). The endpoint radio maintains an RSSI-to-MCS mapping that selects the appropriate modulation, coding rate, and spatial streams to maximize throughput without packet loss. As signal quality degrades, the MCS index drops, reducing the data rate. Performance also varies by protocol; for instance, 802.11g and 802.11n may exhibit comparable performance at certain RSSI levels, though 802.11n typically suffers higher initial loss due to complex modulation. In environments with poor RSSI, 802.11g might outperform 802.11n for latency-sensitive applications like VoIP, whereas high-speed data transfer requires newer technologies like 802.11n or 802.11ac.

text: To retrieve upload and download utilization for a specific port (e.g., GigabitEthernet2/23) on a Cisco Catalyst 6500 series router with a 720 Supervisor card via SNMP, utilize the OLD-CISCO-INTERFACES-MIB. This MIB provides OIDs that correspond to the 'input rate' and 'output rate' values displayed in the output of the `show interface` command. To query the specific interface, replace the variable 'x' in the OID with the interface index obtained from the command `show snmp mib ifmib ifindex`. Note that while the MIB documentation describes these values as a 'five-minute exponentially-decayed moving average,' the actual reported rate aligns with the `load-interval` configuration on the interface (defaulting to 5 minutes). This method offers a point-in-time measurement of interface utilization without requiring an external system to calculate deltas between polling intervals, though the exponentially-decayed nature may reduce accuracy compared to instantaneous measurements.

text: In High-Performance Computing (HPC) interconnect networks such as InfiniBand and OmniPath, the term 'N½ value' refers to the network's bisection width. A bisection divides the network into two equal halves, each containing N/2 end-devices. The bisection width measures the bandwidth available to cross this cut, representing the over-subscription ratio between the cumulative access bandwidth on one side and the bandwidth capacity of the links connecting the two sides. Ideally, a 1:1 ratio exists between access bandwidth and interconnect bandwidth; however, achieving this is often prohibitively expensive or topologically impossible. Understanding bisection width is critical for analyzing network over-subscription and ensuring performance expectations align with physical constraints.

text:
In a small business network topology utilizing a pfSense firewall and downstream unmanaged Netgear ProSafe switches, network congestion can occur during high-bandwidth activities such as internet streaming, large file transfers, or internal file server access. To mitigate this, consider the following strategies:

1. **Hardware Upgrades**: Replace unmanaged switches with managed switches (e.g., Cisco SG300 series, Arista, Juniper, or Brocade) to enable advanced traffic monitoring, Quality of Service (QoS) policies, and better troubleshooting capabilities.
2. **Traffic Isolation**: Utilize tools like Robocopy on client machines to limit bandwidth usage for large file transfers.
3. **Layer 2 Behavior**: Understand that unmanaged Layer 2 switches forward traffic between connected ports (e.g., Workstation A to Workstation B) without contacting upstream devices like the firewall. Traffic remains local to the switch segment unless it traverses a router or firewall interface.

For troubleshooting, isolate issues by testing components independently and utilizing network analysis tools provided by the firewall and switch vendors.

text: Marketing claims of 'dedicated' 1 Gbps Ethernet Dedicated Internet Access (EDIA) services from cable Hybrid Fiber-Coaxial (HFC) providers to every address nationwide are often instances of marketing hyperbole rather than technical reality. While providers may deliver gigabit speeds from the subscriber premises to the first provider equipment (the demarcation point), the actual throughput to external content is rarely guaranteed at that level due to shared infrastructure constraints. True dedicated symmetrical gigabit service typically requires fiber optics or specialized carrier-grade infrastructure (e.g., ATM OC-12), which is often cost-prohibitive to install in remote or rural locations due to high buildout charges. Consequently, advertised 'dedicated' speeds may refer only to the local loop performance, not the end-to-end path capacity.

text:
ISP Bandwidth Throttling Mechanism

When a user is subscribed to a capped bandwidth plan (e.g., 16 Mbps), the ISP does not download data from the source (e.g., Facebook) at a reduced speed and then transmit it at the cap. Instead, the ISP's infrastructure transmits data to the user as fast as possible. The bandwidth limit is enforced on the user's link via packet dropping mechanisms.

**Mechanism:**
1. The remote server (e.g., Facebook) attempts to send data aggressively.
2. The ISP's router monitors the transmission rate. If a packet would cause the transmission to exceed the user's 16 Mbps limit, the router drops that packet.
3. The TCP protocol detects the missing packet and requests retransmission.
4. The server interprets the packet loss as network congestion and automatically reduces its transmission rate (TCP Sliding Window adjustment).
5. The system settles at a rate that does not exceed the 16 Mbps cap.

**Key Concept:** The ISP does not cache files or pre-download them. The limitation is enforced dynamically by dropping packets to signal the sender to slow down, ensuring the user's link is never exceeded.

text:
ISP Bandwidth Throttling Mechanism (Alternative Explanation)

In a capped bandwidth scenario, the ISP does not download the file from the source. The download is performed by the user's client. The ISP acts merely as a transmitter.

**Process:**
1. Data transfer from the source to the ISP is typically faster than the user's 16 Mbps cap.
2. Packets are queued and transmitted to the user at the capped rate (16 Mbps).
3. The user's browser sends ACKs for received packets.
4. The server continues sending more data based on these ACKs.

**Conclusion:** The ISP does not limit the upstream speed from the source; it limits the downstream speed to the user by controlling the transmission rate on the final hop.

text:
Network Transmission Speed and Packet Loss

The speed of transmission is determined by the interaction between the sender (e.g., Facebook) and the receiver (e.g., User PC). Network devices (routers, switches) transmit packets as fast as possible.

**Congestion Control:**
If congestion occurs or packets are dropped between the sender and receiver, the receiving part informs the sending part to slow down and retransmit lost packets. This feedback loop ensures that the effective data rate matches the available path capacity.

text:
Bandwidth Measurement via ICMPv6 Ping

**Context:** Analyzing `tcpdump` data from a ping test to derive bits per second.

**Command Used:**
```bash
sudo ping6 fd9c:b4f9:3108:1045:213:5003:59:324e -c 10 -i .31725 -s 1380
```

**Packet Structure Analysis:**
Based on the `tcpdump` output, the total bits per packet on the wire are calculated as follows:
- Payload length: 1388 bytes
- ICMPv6 Overhead: 8 bytes
- IPv6 Header: 40 bytes (assumed untagged)
- Ethernet Header: 38 bytes (assumed untagged)
- **Total Packet Size:** 1474 bytes = 11,792 bits

**Limitations of Ping for Bandwidth Testing:**
Ping does not usually saturate the channel. Consequently, measuring bandwidth using ICMP ping will yield a result significantly lower than the actual network bandwidth capacity. For accurate saturation testing, larger payloads and continuous transmission are required.

text:
Multi-Interface Bandwidth Aggregation

**Scenario:** Connecting a single host to a network using multiple Ethernet cables (e.g., 30 cables) to increase upload speed.

**Feasibility:**
- **General Hosts:** No. Each interface is generally treated as a separate network. Simply connecting multiple cables does not automatically aggregate bandwidth.
- **Servers:** Some servers support Link Aggregation (LAG) or EtherChannel, bonding multiple connections (typically max 8) into a single virtual link.

**Performance Considerations:**
1. **Packet Loss and Order:** Dividing a single network flow across multiple links can cause packet loss and out-of-order delivery, degrading performance and breaking real-time applications (e.g., VoIP).
2. **Flow Limitation:** If a single flow is assigned to a single link, the bandwidth increase is only realized if multiple distinct flows are present. Each individual flow remains limited to the speed of a single link.
3. **Switch Constraints:** Aggregation is controlled by vendor algorithms. Performance is often optimized if the number of links is a power of two (2, 4, 8).
4. **Network Oversubscription:** If a host has multiple high-speed links (e.g., four 1 Gb links) but the switch has a single bottleneck link (e.g., one 1 Gb link) toward the destination, the network is oversubscribed. The switch will drop frames, causing packet loss despite the host's multiple connections.

text:
The term 'bandwidth' has distinct definitions depending on the context:
1. **Radio Frequency Context**: Refers to the range of frequencies within a specific band. For example, legacy WiFi (802.11 b/g) operates in the 2.4 - 2.5 GHz range, resulting in a 0.1 GHz bandwidth.
2. **Network Context**: Refers to the maximum amount of information that can be transferred per second on a specific medium using a specific protocol.

**Relationship**: While loosely connected, the two definitions are related through radio technology. Generally, a wider radio bandwidth allows for a higher potential data rate. However, network bandwidth can also be improved without increasing radio bandwidth by enhancing the protocol efficiency. For instance, WiFi standards evolved from 2 Mbps (original spec) to 11 Mbps (802.11b) and 54 Mbps (802.11g) while maintaining the same underlying radio bandwidth, demonstrating that protocol improvements can increase data rates independently of spectrum width.

metadata.title: Does bandwidth limit apply only from my ISP to my network?

text:
The relationship between frequency bandwidth and data rate is defined by modulation. The correct definition of bandwidth in this context is "a range of frequencies occupied by a modulated carrier."

When a carrier wave is modulated to transmit data, the frequency of the modulating signal corresponds to the data rate. Consequently, a higher data rate requires a higher bandwidth for the signal. While coding algorithms or multiple sub-carriers can minimize this increase, the fundamental principle remains: increasing the data rate increases the occupied bandwidth. Conversely, for a fixed bandwidth and modulation technique, there is a maximum achievable data rate. Therefore, when discussing the bandwidth of a data circuit, the term refers to the maximum data rate possible given the available signal bandwidth (electrical or optical).

text:
**Problem**: Ingress traffic policing and shaping are often misunderstood when the next-hop device (e.g., an ISP router) has no control over the traffic. A common concern is that policing ingress traffic on a firewall's WAN interface only causes queuing or dropping packets, forcing external senders to retransmit and ultimately consuming more bandwidth over time.

**Solution**: Traffic shaping is essential for Quality of Service (QoS) on shared links. For example, if a WAN link carries both SIP trunk traffic and general Internet downloads, uncontrolled download traffic can overload the link and cause voice packets to be dropped.

**Explanation**: Ingress traffic shaping can be implemented by manipulating the TCP handshake. By holding back TCP ACKs, the firewall can artificially limit the amount of data the remote sender transmits, effectively shaping the ingress flow to prioritize critical traffic like voice without requiring control over the external sender or the ISP router.

metadata.title: How can I calculate the network throughput using ping responses?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Accurately distinguishes between shaping (outbound) and policing (inbound/outbound) and correctly explains the TCP retransmission penalty for dropped ingress packets.
metadata.context_summary: Reconciles the dual definitions of 'bandwidth' in wireless contexts: frequency range vs. data transfer rate.

text: Ingress traffic policing and shaping are distinct from egress traffic management and serve different purposes. While shaping is typically applied to outbound traffic to allocate specific bandwidth percentages or rates to different flows, policing can be applied to both ingress and egress traffic, though it is most effective on egress. Policing ingress traffic involves dropping packets; for TCP protocols, this causes the receiver to miss segments, fail to send ACKs, and force the sender to retransmit data. While this retransmission consumes bandwidth over a longer period, it effectively reduces the incoming data rate, allowing other inbound traffic to utilize available bandwidth. Conversely, for connectionless protocols like UDP, policing ingress traffic is the primary method to prevent a single host from monopolizing inbound bandwidth. It is crucial to distinguish between bandwidth (maximum link capacity per second) and data usage (total volume sent/received over time); policing reduces the rate of data usage but does not alter the physical link's bandwidth capacity.

metadata.title: Would multiple connections to a network make the connection faster?

text: ISPs track subscriber data usage and enforce speed limits using logical subscriber interfaces rather than relying solely on physical interface queuing. In PPPoE environments, each subscriber is assigned a dedicated logical interface that tracks bytes in/out and applies individual policers based on usage quotas. Similarly, in IPoE environments (typically using DHCP), logical interfaces are created dynamically per IP address upon DHCP transaction, allowing filters to be applied once quotas are exceeded. While NetFlow/SFlow can track bandwidth by IP address, these methods generally lack the scalability required for large subscriber bases. Effective implementation of these tracking and shaping mechanisms requires routers that support hierarchical queueing, enabling per-subscriber (per-IP) queuing rather than just per-sub-interface or per-physical-interface queuing.

metadata.title: How do cable internet providers limit user bandwith?
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Contains significant inaccuracies regarding ISP architecture (claiming DPI engines sit inline with 500Gbps capacity in every scenario) and oversimplifies the complexity of mobile network subscriber tracking.
metadata.problem_summary: User questions the utility of shaping/policing ingress traffic on a WAN interface when the upstream device is uncontrolled, fearing it only causes retransmissions.

text: ISPs manage subscriber identification and traffic shaping through Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) engines positioned inline at network aggregation points, often between edge routers to capture traffic before NAT or firewalls. Subscriber identification relies on various methods: in cable networks, the public IP address is the primary identifier; in campus networks, MAC addresses are used via DHCP requests; and in mobile networks, shorter DHCP leases and diverse connection points necessitate external provisioning systems using RADIUS feeds or DHCP snooping to recognize subscribers within the first few kilobytes of traffic. Complex networks may identify subscribers by VLAN+IP pairs, L2TP tunnel IDs, or MPLS labels. Once identified, DPI engines can shape throughput, packet rates, or connection rates per second. Shaping mechanisms include modifying TCP window sizes (less clean, packet rewriting) or, more commonly, queueing packets and transmitting them in a controlled manner or dropping them if queues exceed configured maximums. Algorithms such as 'Blue' are often used to buffer packets proactively before maximum throughput is reached to handle traffic bursts. While ISPs generally avoid shaping traffic between their own edge routers to respect link speeds, they utilize shaping internally to ensure fair sharing among hosts, prioritize critical applications like VoIP and gaming, and prevent bandwidth monopolization by heavy users like BitTorrent clients.

text: Increasing the packet error rate in a TCP simulation leads to increased bandwidth consumption due to TCP's reliability mechanisms. As a reliable transport protocol, TCP requests the retransmission of any segments that are lost or corrupted during transmission. Consequently, when the error rate rises, the volume of data sent increases as the protocol attempts to compensate for the lost segments, resulting in higher overall bandwidth usage.

text: Utilizing a high-speed network interface (e.g., Gigabit Ethernet) with a slower internal bus (e.g., 80 MBps) provides significant advantages over slower NICs, even if the full link speed cannot be achieved. Historically, when 1 Gb NICs were introduced, server buses often limited throughput to approximately 400 Mb, which was still four times faster than 100 Mb networks. As bus speeds have evolved to match or exceed NIC capabilities, the bottleneck has diminished. Therefore, a high-speed NIC is beneficial as long as the system bus allows for throughput superior to that of a slower NIC, even if it does not fully saturate the NIC's maximum potential.

text:
TCP and standard packet-switched networks (including application, transport, and link layers) do not inherently provide bandwidth or delay guarantees. In packet-switched environments, all packets compete for bandwidth on congested links, leading to queuing and eventual packet drops. Intermediate devices (routers and switches) operate in a stateless manner, processing packets individually without maintaining logical connection state.

To achieve bandwidth or delay guarantees, a continuous channel must be arbitrated and established, a characteristic of circuit-switched networks rather than packet-switched ones. While Quality of Service (QoS) prioritization schemes can offer guarantees if correctly configured, the primary method for ensuring bandwidth is increasing available capacity until congestion is eliminated. If budget constraints prevent over-provisioning, QoS mechanisms are utilized to manage traffic.

text:
Measuring maximum LAN bandwidth usage requires distinguishing between average throughput and theoretical limits. Wireshark can calculate average Layer 2/3/4 throughput over a specific time period by summing frame, packet, or payload sizes and dividing by the duration; however, it is not ideal for capturing momentary peak throughput. For serious measurement of instantaneous throughput, network switch functions such as RMON (per interface), sFlow, or OpenFlow (filterable by source, destination, port, VLAN, size) are superior.

The theoretical maximum bandwidth of a path is determined by the bottleneck link—the lowest bandwidth among all links in the path.

text:
To measure maximum bandwidth between two LAN endpoints, the `iperf` tool is recommended for a simple and effective method. Alternatively, Wireshark can be used to measure the throughput of a specific TCP session by following these steps:
1. Open Wireshark and start a packet capture.
2. Initiate a file download or transfer between the target PCs.
3. Stop the capture once the transfer is complete.
4. Apply the filter: `ip.addr eq <source_ip> and ip.addr eq <destination_ip>`.
5. Right-click any packet in the filtered list and select 'Follow' > 'TCP Stream'.
6. Navigate to the 'Statistics' menu, select 'TCP Stream Graphs', and choose 'Throughput'.
A new window will display the throughput graph for that specific session.

text: Network device interfaces (e.g., router web interfaces) and client applications (e.g., web browsers) typically display raw data rates rather than 'net throughput' excluding errors. Throughput is defined as the rate at which data transfers through the media, regardless of packet integrity. This metric includes both good packets and corrupt packets (e.g., those with failed checksums) that require retransmission. Consequently, a reported throughput figure represents the total bit rate on the link, not the successfully delivered data rate. This distinction is critical for troubleshooting sporadic quality issues, such as video stream degradation, where external interference may cause packet corruption despite stable raw throughput.

text: In computing, bandwidth represents the maximum theoretical rate of data transfer across a path (bidirectional). When monitoring data transfer rates, the reported figures generally include all packets traversing the link, irrespective of their validity. Corrupt packets, such as those with failed checksums, are counted in the throughput measurement. Therefore, the displayed rate reflects the total volume of data moving through the medium, including retransmissions and errors, rather than only successfully delivered 'good' packets.

text: Network devices and software interfaces typically display link speed or throughput metrics that differ significantly from application-level 'goodput'. For wireless connections, such as 802.11, the displayed rate often corresponds to the PHY bandwidth derived from the Modulation and Coding Scheme (MCS) index. This index incorporates variables including channel width, spatial streams, modulation, and coding rates to determine the theoretical maximum bandwidth between the access point and the client. This metric represents the raw data rate capability of the physical layer and is distinct from the actual throughput observed by end-user applications, which must account for protocol overhead and potential packet corruption. In wireless environments, overhead is higher than in wired connections, resulting in lower application throughput. Consequently, a stable throughput reading on a router interface does not guarantee consistent video quality if the underlying link experiences intermittent corruption due to external interference, as the displayed rate may not reflect the loss of packets requiring retransmission.

text: In telecommunications, the terms 'band', 'broadband', and 'baseband' refer to specific characteristics of the electromagnetic spectrum used for transmission. A 'band' applies to a specific range of frequencies within the spectrum. 'Broadband' utilizes a broad range of frequencies, as opposed to narrowband. 'Baseband' refers to a signal band starting at zero frequency (or close to it). 'Bandwidth' is a dual-purpose term: it describes the spectral width of a channel (e.g., 100 MHz) and the amount of data that can be transported within a given time frame (e.g., 100 megabytes per second). The data throughput definition is an extension of the original spectral meaning, derived from the correlation between frequency width and data capacity.

text:
To monitor traffic and bandwidth consumption on a trunk port of a Cisco WS-C3650-48TS L3 switch, administrators can utilize the `show interfaces` command. This command provides real-time statistics, including the 5-minute input and output bandwidth rates, along with error counters. For example, the output for interface GigabitEthernet3/0/2 displays the current bit/sec rates and packet counts. To visualize this data over time, Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) can be configured with a Read-Only community string, allowing tools like MRTG or PRTG to poll the interface and generate traffic graphs. While IP accounting and NetFlow can monitor individual traffic flows on ports, they may not function correctly on L3 switches if the traffic is not process-switched. The `show interfaces` command output includes critical fields such as MTU, bandwidth (BW), delay (DLY), and various error counters (CRC, runts, giants) to assess interface health and utilization.

text: In circuit switching, the network reserves a fixed amount of bandwidth for each connection along its path for the duration of the session. The design bandwidth of the network must be sufficient to support the maximum number of simultaneous connections (N) multiplied by the required bandwidth per connection (L), expressed as L * N. When a new connection is established, it allocates its reserved bandwidth; if the network lacks sufficient capacity, the new connection cannot be established. Crucially, new connections do not interfere with already established ones because the bandwidth is pre-allocated. The actual data transmission rate of a single connection is irrelevant to the network's reservation; the network guarantees the reserved capacity regardless of whether the connection utilizes it fully. This contrasts with packet switching, where bandwidth is shared dynamically and only actually transmitted data consumes resources. A major drawback of circuit switching is the inefficiency of reserving fixed bandwidth, as illustrated by voice traffic where statistical multiplexing could allow for significantly lower total network capacity (e.g., 24 Gbit/s instead of 64 Gbit/s for 1 million calls) due to pauses and turn-taking. Packet switching offers superior scalability and simpler, often stateless, forwarding nodes, though it lacks inherent bandwidth guarantees for real-time applications without Quality of Service (QoS) mechanisms.

text:
In network diagnostics, it is critical to distinguish between bandwidth, latency, and throughput, as well as understand the specific metrics reported by different tools. 

Definitions:
- **Bandwidth**: The maximum theoretical data transfer rate of a cable or link (the size of the pipe).
- **Latency**: The time delay introduced by network elements (how fast cars travel), often measured in milliseconds (ms).
- **Throughput**: The actual amount of data successfully transferred over a given time period.

Tool Comparisons:
- **Ping**: Measures latency (round-trip time) using ICMP packets.
- **Iperf**: Measures bandwidth/throughput between two endpoints by configuring test parameters on the hosts.
- **Ookla Speedtest**: Reports download and upload speeds by measuring throughput to external servers.

Discrepancy Explanation:
A scenario where Ping reports 20ms latency and Iperf reports 20Mbps bandwidth, while Ookla Speedtest reports 80Mbps download and 15Mbps upload, arises because these tools measure different aspects and paths. Speedtest measures throughput to a specific external server (which may be congested or geographically distant), whereas Iperf measures the capacity of the local link or path between configured hosts. Throughput is analogous to the number of truckloads delivered; Speedtest measures the load to a warehouse, while Iperf measures the load to the final destination. To verify speeds with tools like Iperf or `nc` to match Speedtest results, one must ensure the test destination and traffic patterns mirror the external service being tested.

text:
Clarification on Network Metrics: Bandwidth, Latency, Download Speed, and Upload Speed.

- **Bandwidth**: The maximum possible data transfer rate over a cable in a unit of time under ideal circumstances (no contention, collisions, etc.). It represents the theoretical capacity of the link.
- **Latency**: The slowness or delay introduced into the network. Factors contributing to latency include router congestion (packets waiting in queues) and server processing delays (e.g., Google servers being clogged). Latency is variable and changes continuously based on network conditions.
- **Speeds (Throughput)**: The eventual data transfer rate achieved. This is largely controlled by network providers and is determined by Quality of Service (QoS) parameters that dictate how packets are serviced. Upload and download speeds are the result of specific Service Level Agreements (SLAs) with data providers.

text:
For an individual attempting to stream video to an international audience from a home network, scalability and network restrictions are primary constraints. The quality and cost economics of video distribution rely heavily on placing large content caches near large populations of viewers rather than near the source. Live streaming requires encoding engines positioned optimally for the viewer's output format.

Key Considerations:
- **Content Distribution Networks (CDNs)**: Essential for handling mass viewership. YouTube and AWS provide documentation on encoding mechanisms and CDN architectures suitable for this purpose.
- **Scalability**: There is a significant difference between a mass event (millions of viewers) and a narrow broadcast (e.g., quarterly investor report to hundreds of financial centers). The latter is more feasible for an average home network with appropriate hardware/software, but mass streaming requires enterprise-grade infrastructure and CDN integration to manage traffic levels and avoid provider restrictions.

text:
In network modeling where users are nodes connected via the internet, link scoring can be based on features such as throughput or distance. A common metric for grading link quality is the **bandwidth-distance product**, as high bandwidth and long distance are often traded off against each other; their product serves as a good measure of the achievement grade.

Regarding the relationship between throughput, distance, and packet loss:
Graphs showing packet loss increasing with throughput often illustrate the relationship between packet loss and distance for various throughput scenarios, rather than a direct causal link where higher throughput inherently causes higher packet loss. Throughput is a positive aspect of performance, but the observed increase in packet loss in such graphs is typically a function of distance-induced constraints interacting with the specific throughput level selected for the scenario.

text: Minimizing maximum link utilization is a critical strategy for network optimization, congestion control, and Quality of Service (QoS). While maximizing bandwidth usage is desirable, operating links at constant maximum utilization leaves no spare capacity for unexpected events or traffic spikes. By lowering peak utilization, network administrators create 'headroom,' which extends the time before network upgrades are required and provides resilience against sudden load increases. This approach allows for more even load distribution, reducing the risk of congestion and lowering overall operational costs compared to networks that are perpetually saturated.

text: The concept of minimizing maximum link utilization focuses on managing network headroom—the difference between a link's maximum capacity and its current utilization. This headroom represents the buffer available for future growth, additional workloads, or unexpected traffic bursts. A smaller headroom shortens the time before network upgrades become necessary. To optimize network longevity and cost-efficiency, administrators should aim to distribute load evenly across the network. This strategy lowers peak utilization rates while maintaining overall throughput, thereby extending the operational lifespan of the current infrastructure without requiring immediate hardware upgrades.

text: Detecting available bandwidth on a link without inducing congestion presents challenges, as active testing tools like iperf inherently congest the network to measure throughput. Passive detection methods include querying local device specifications (e.g., `wmic NIC get description,speed` on Windows) for theoretical capacity. For path bandwidth, extraction from routing protocols is possible within controlled internal networks, though Internet paths often require active testing. In practice, the TCP protocol dynamically manages effective bandwidth along a path based on current traffic conditions, often rendering explicit bandwidth detection unnecessary for application functionality. However, effective bandwidth fluctuates continuously depending on the overall traffic load.

Such methods yield approximations rather than precise measurements and are practical only in networks with full visibility and control.

text:
Server Internet Bandwidth Impact on Client Download Speeds

A server's Internet bandwidth directly limits the aggregate download speed available to its clients. The limiting factor is the server's upload capacity, not its download capacity, as the server must push data out to clients.

**Mechanism**:
Even if clients possess high-speed WAN connections (e.g., 1 Gbps each), the total throughput is capped by the server's maximum upload capacity. For example, if a server has a 15 Mbps upload link and 10 clients attempt to download simultaneously, the total available bandwidth is 15 Mbps. In an ideal scenario, this results in 1.5 Mbps per client, regardless of the clients' individual connection speeds.

**Conclusion**:
The server's upload speed acts as the bottleneck for client downloads. If the aggregate demand from clients exceeds the server's upload capacity, the server slows down the clients' download speeds proportionally.

text:
Limiting VLAN Traffic on Cisco IOS (Ingress vs. Egress)

**Problem**:
Limiting the bandwidth usage of a specific internal VLAN to a target rate (e.g., 2 Mbps) on a Cisco router (IOS 15.3) when the WAN link is 10 Mbps. The concern is that if a server within the VLAN sends data at a high rate back to the Internet, the router may fail to prevent the WAN link from saturating before traffic policing can occur.

**Solution & Explanation**:
1.  **Ingress Control Limitation**: There is limited control over traffic entering the network from the Internet. By the time traffic is visible to the router, the WAN bandwidth has already been consumed. At this stage, the router can only **police** the traffic (drop excess packets) rather than prevent the saturation that occurred upstream.
2.  **TCP Reaction**: If the traffic utilizes TCP connections, dropping packets triggers retransmission requests. This causes the sender to slow down, but this is an inexact and reactive method of bandwidth control.
3.  **Egress Control**: To effectively limit the rate at which the VLAN sends data *out* to the Internet, traffic shaping or policing must be applied on the egress interface (the interface facing the WAN) or via QoS policies that match the VLAN traffic before it exits the router. Simply dropping traffic after the link is saturated is ineffective for preventing the saturation itself.

text:
Troubleshooting Internet Bandwidth Saturation

**Problem**:
Sudden peak bandwidth utilization on a 20 Mbps Internet lease line causing network slowness. The root cause of the traffic spike is unknown.

**Solution**:
Capture all network traffic traversing the Internet connection to identify the source and type of traffic causing the saturation.

**Explanation**:
Effective troubleshooting requires visibility into the traffic flow. Without knowing what specific traffic is consuming the bandwidth, no targeted action can be taken to mitigate the issue. 

**Implementation**:
Perform a packet capture (sniff) on the network segment connecting to the Internet. The specific method depends on the network hardware and topology (e.g., using a span port, a dedicated port mirror, or a hardware tap). Once the capture data is analyzed, the specific application or protocol causing the spike can be identified and addressed.

text:
Cisco EtherChannel Configuration and Limits

**Interface Grouping and Bandwidth**:
- **Grouping**: Multiple interfaces can be grouped into an EtherChannel (Link Aggregation) to increase bandwidth and provide redundancy. The number of interfaces per group depends on the switch model and interface type (e.g., up to 8 Ethernet interfaces per module on Catalyst 4500/4000).
- **Bandwidth Verification**: Used bandwidth of an EtherChannel group can be checked using the `show interfaces port-channel` command. The output displays the total bandwidth (BW) of the logical channel and load statistics.

**Sample Output Analysis**:
`show interfaces port-channel 1` displays metrics including:
- `BW`: Total bandwidth of the channel (e.g., 400000 Kbit for a 4-port FastEthernet channel).
- `load`: Current load relative to capacity.
- `input/output rate`: Traffic rates over the last 5 minutes.

**Maximum EtherChannel Limits by Platform**:
- **Catalyst 6500/6000 (CatOS)**: Max 126 channels (6/9-slot chassis) or 63 channels (13-slot chassis).
- **Catalyst 6500 (Cisco IOS)**: Max 128 channels (Release 12.2(18)SXE+); Max 64 channels (earlier releases).
- **Catalyst 4500/4000 (Cisco IOS)**: Max 64 channels.
- **Catalyst 3750**: Max 12 channels (Release 12.1); Max 48 channels (Release 12.2+) in a stack.
- **Catalyst 2900XL/3500XL**: Max 12 port groups.
- **Catalyst 2970**: Max 12 EtherChannels.
- **Catalyst 2940**: Max 6 EtherChannels (8 ports per channel).
- **Catalyst Express 500**: Max 6 Fast or Gigabit EtherChannel groups.
- **Catalyst 1900/2820**: Max 2 port FECs.
- **Catalyst 2948G-L3 / 4908G-L3**: Supports up to 16 FECs (4 ports per channel) or 4 GECs respectively.
- **Catalyst 2950/2955**: Max 6 port groups (source/destination basis).
- **Catalyst 8500 Series**: Supports up to 4 port FECs as a Layer 3 forwarding path.

text:
High-Frequency Interface Utilization Monitoring on Cisco Nexus 5000

**Context**:
Requirement to measure interface utilization on Cisco Nexus 5000 switches and fabric extenders at sub-second or 1-second intervals for network simulation research.

**Evaluated Methods**:
1.  **SNMP (IF-MIB)**: Querying byte counters via SNMP is insufficient because counters update irregularly (approx. every 10 seconds), and SNMP response latency distorts timing accuracy.
2.  **NETCONF/XML**: Querying via NETCONF is too slow to support the required high-frequency polling intervals.
3.  **ERSPAN**: Capturing traffic via ERSPAN to a management station allows for packet counting based on VLAN tags. However, this introduces overhead, requires additional infrastructure, and may suffer from forwarding limitations (e.g., Nexus forwarding ~7GB/s of mirrored traffic).

**Recommended Solution**:
**Physical Link Tapping**:
- **Optical Tap**: Use an in-line optical tap to split the fiber connection to a secondary cable for monitoring.
- **Copper Tap**: Use a powered copper tap for copper links.

**Advantages**:
- Provides an instantly accurate, infinitely granular picture of traffic crossing the wire.
- Bypasses switch processing overhead and counter update delays.

**Alternative (SPAN)**:
If tapping all links is impractical, SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) can be used to mirror all ports to a single high-speed interface (10G or 40G). 
- **Caveats**: Adds processing time and risk of traffic drops due to oversubscription ratios. Accuracy depends on the specific requirements and available bandwidth on the destination port.

text: On Cisco Nexus 5000 series switches and fabric extenders, there is no native support for sub-second or fine-grained interface utilization measurement via standard polling mechanisms. Attempts to use IF-MIB for byte counters result in updates occurring approximately every 10 seconds, which is insufficient for high-frequency analysis. Additionally, SNMP polling intervals can be irregular under low load conditions, distorting utilization calculations. Netconf/XML agents on these platforms are also too slow to support second-level polling intervals. While ERSPAN offers a method to capture traffic and calculate utilization externally, it introduces significant overhead and potential forwarding limitations (e.g., handling ~7GB/s) on the Nexus device. An alternative approach observed on Cisco 6500 and 7600 platforms involves using RMON history to store fine-grained data, which can then be processed by external scripts (e.g., using snmpbulkwalk and RRDtool) to generate accurate utilization graphs. Note that RMON history is not supported on Nexus 5000 devices.

text: When summarizing network interface utilization metrics (ifinutilization and ifoututilization), two primary methods are used: averaging and finding the maximum. Calculating the average involves summing all sampled values and dividing by the count. This method provides a good estimation of overall data volume but may obscure traffic bursts and congestion if the load is uneven. Averaging is effectively the same as low-frequency sampling (less than one sample per minute). Finding the maximum involves identifying the highest utilization value recorded and discarding others. This method accurately represents peak loads but does not reflect overall data volume or average load. To effectively identify peaks using the maximum method, a high sampling frequency (significantly more than one sample per minute) is required.

text: Port utilization calculations on Nokia/ALU switches differ from simple L2 octet-based formulas because the device reports Layer 2 statistics (ifin/out octets) which exclude Layer 1 overhead. The reported octet counts do not include the preamble, Start of Frame Delimiter (SFD), and Inter-Frame Gaps (IFG), which total 20 bytes per packet on the wire. Consequently, the standard formula `(octets * 8) / capacity` yields incorrect results. To calculate accurate utilization for a port (e.g., 1000Mbps), the formula must account for the L1 overhead: `8 * (octets + (packets * 20)) / capacity`. For example, if the reported octets are 1,185,742 and packets are 10,264, the calculation `(1,185,742 + (10,264 * 20)) * 8 / 1,000,000,000` correctly yields the reported utilization percentage.

text: In Linux Traffic Control (tc) configurations using HTB (Hierarchical Token Bucket), the `default` parameter in the root qdisc determines the class assigned to new IP addresses that do not match any existing flow filters. In the provided configuration, the command `tc qdisc add dev br-lan root handle 1: htb default 10` explicitly sets class 1:10 as the default. Any traffic not matched by the subsequent `tc filter` rules (which assign specific IPs to classes 1:10, 1:11, or 1:12) will be automatically assigned to class 1:10. To change the default behavior, the `default` parameter in the root qdisc command must be modified to point to the desired class ID (e.g., `default 12` to assign unmatched traffic to the 250kbit class).

text:
Network Bandwidth and Oversubscription

The bandwidth of a link to an ISP (e.g., 100 Mbps) represents the maximum capacity between the customer's router and the ISP's router. It is critical to distinguish between link capacity and per-device availability, particularly regarding traffic direction and network topology.

Outbound Traffic and Oversubscription:
For outbound traffic, the effective bandwidth per computer is determined by the link speed from the computer to the next device (e.g., access switch) and the uplink capacity of that switch. Networks often employ oversubscription, where the aggregate bandwidth of access ports exceeds the uplink bandwidth to the aggregation layer.

Example Calculation:
If an access switch has twenty-four 1 Gbps ports and a single 1 Gbps uplink, the oversubscription ratio is 24:1. This does not mean each computer is limited to 1/24th of the uplink speed. Instead, if only one computer transmits at 1 Gbps, it can utilize the full uplink. However, if all 24 computers attempt to transmit simultaneously, traffic will be dropped due to buffer limitations. Switches typically use first-come, first-served queuing with very small buffers, leading to packet loss under heavy load.

Protocol Impact:
The impact of dropped packets depends on the protocol:
- UDP: Packets are lost without retransmission, degrading application performance.
- TCP: The protocol requests retransmission of lost segments, which can increase latency and reduce effective throughput.

Inbound Traffic and Security:
Inbound traffic from the Internet is rarely oversubscribed and is generally asymmetric (inbound volume often exceeds outbound). Administrators have limited control over incoming traffic. A single request from an internal host can trigger a massive response from an external host, potentially monopolizing the inbound ISP connection. This vulnerability is exploited in Denial of Service (DoS) attacks. Quality of Service (QoS) mechanisms can prioritize real-time traffic (e.g., VoIP) but cannot fully mitigate uncontrolled inbound floods.

Note: The simplistic calculation of dividing total ISP bandwidth by the number of computers (e.g., 100 Mbps / 200 computers = 0.5 Mbps) is incorrect because it ignores internal switch uplink capacities, oversubscription ratios, and traffic directionality.

text:
Ethernet Speed Standards and Bitrate Scaling

Network interface speeds (e.g., 10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps) often increase by factors of 10, unlike memory storage which increases by powers of 2. This pattern is driven by engineering convenience and historical standards rather than binary addressing constraints.

Key Distinctions:
- Memory Storage: Increases by powers of 2 because memory is addressed using binary (base-2) addresses.
- Ethernet/Wireless: Speeds increased by powers of 10 as a convenient marketing and standardization goal, though this is not a strict rule.

Exceptions and Modern Standards:
- Ethernet: While 10, 100, and 1000 Mbps were standard, modern standards include 40 Gbps and 100 Gbps.
- Wi-Fi: Speeds do not follow a linear 10x increase. Examples include:
  - 802.11b: 11 Mbps
  - 802.11a: 54 Mbps
  - 802.11g: 54 Mbps
  - 802.11n: 150 Mbps per stream (and higher with MIMO)
- Optical Modules (SFP): Speeds include 1 Gbps, 2.5 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 25 Gbps, 40 Gbps, 100 Gbps, and 400 Gbps.

text:
Network Interface Card (NIC) and Transceiver Speed Evolution

While early networking equipment adhered to powers of 10 (10, 100, 1000 Mbps), modern developments show a more varied progression in supported bitrates. Examination of Small Form-factor Pluggable (SFP) transceiver standards reveals speeds such as 1 Gbps, 2.5 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 25 Gbps, 40 Gbps, 100 Gbps, and 400 Gbps. This indicates that while powers of 10 remain common, intermediate speeds (e.g., 2.5 Gbps) are now standard to optimize cost and performance for specific use cases.

metadata.problem_summary: User asks if network bandwidth is divided equally among all connected computers (e.g., 100Mbps ISP link shared by 200 computers results in 0.5Mbps each).

text:
Relationship Between Bandwidth, Frequency, and Bitrate

Bandwidth and data rate are directly related through modulation frequency. Increasing bandwidth allows for the use of higher modulation frequencies, which in turn increases the data rate.

Core Concept:
- Bandwidth is a function of the modulation frequency used to transmit data.
- If the modulation frequency remains constant, the bandwidth remains constant.

Misconception Clarification:
It is incorrect to assume that simply increasing the bandwidth range (e.g., shifting the spectrum from 1-5 MHz to 2-10 MHz) while keeping the lowest frequency constant will increase the data rate. The data rate increases only when the bandwidth is expanded by utilizing higher modulation frequencies. Therefore, to increase the bitrate, one must increase the bandwidth by employing higher frequency components in the signal spectrum.

text:
Troubleshooting Low 10Gbps Throughput on Windows

Problem:
Two servers connected via a 10 Gbps cable are tested using IPERF. Despite the physical link being 10 Gbps, the measured throughput is only 1.5–1.7 Gbps.

Server Configuration:
- Server A: Windows 8.1 Pro 64-bit
- Server B: Windows 7 Enterprise 64-bit

IPERF Output Analysis:
Testing from Server A (192.168.10.100) to Server B (192.168.10.200) shows consistent bandwidth around 1.7 Gbps. Testing in the reverse direction (Server B to Server A) shows similar results (~1.5 Gbps). The transfer rates are significantly below the 10 Gbps capability.

Solution:
This issue is caused by Windows Auto-Tuning, a feature that limits the TCP receive window size by default to 256 KB. This limitation restricts throughput on high-speed links.

Resolution:
To disable Windows Auto-Tuning and allow the TCP window to scale dynamically, execute the following command on the affected server(s):

netsh int tcp set global autotuninglevel=disabled

Verification:
After disabling Auto-Tuning, re-run the IPERF test. Throughput should approach the full 10 Gbps capacity of the link.

In an ideal world, the default class would be class id 1:12, as shown above, allowing 250kbit bandwidth.
At the moment, any unrecognized device uses the parent class:
tc class add dev br-lan parent 1: classid 1:1 htb rate 2000kbit ceil
Which means they have the ability of taking the entire bandwidth of the network.
I've tried to study the following article: http://lartc.org/howto/lartc.qdisc.classful.html
However, I'm struggling to implement 'leaf classes', could anyone shed some light into this situation? Thanks

tc qdisc add dev br-lan root handle 1: htb default 10

No idea how I previously overlooked this.

text:
Q: I'm not good at English But I really confused about this.
I used the word computer but it can be mobiles or any of devices which can have networking.
Let just the Internet bandwidth is 100Mbps, and It is linked to a router.
There are many switches and hubs which connected to the router.
Eventually, 200 of computers connected to this network by the switches and hubs
So, Is bandwidth of the computer in the network (100/200)Mbps? Which means a maximum of the Internet speed is 0.5Mbps when all of the computers uses the Internet?

[Score:1] This subject is very large, but I will try to cover some key points.
The bandwidth of the link to the ISP is 100 Mbps. That means that your router can send 100 Mb to the ISP's router each second, and the ISP's router can send the same to your router.
You must consider the traffic direction.
For outbound traffic, the bandwidth for each computer is whatever the link speed from the computer to the next device is. For example, your computers could have 1000 Mbps links to the access switches.
The access switches to which the computers connect will have links to some type of aggregation, and those links will have a bandwidth associated with them. The aggregate access bandwidth compared to the uplink bandwidth will give you an oversubscription ratio. For example, if you have a switch with twenty-four 1 Gbps access ports, and a 1 Gbps uplink, you have a 24:1 oversubscription ratio.
You can also calculate an oversubscription ratio to the router, and to the ISP's router.
It is not as simple as dividing the bandwidth of the oversubscribed link between all the computers. For the example of the 24-port switch above, one computer could send 1 Gbps to the aggregation switch if it is the only one sending. If all 24 computers are trying to send 1 Gbps at the same time, a lot of traffic will be dropped. The frames going to the aggregation switch will be sent on a first-come, first-served basis. Switches have very, very small buffers, so the vast majority of the traffic will be dropped.
How that affects things depends on what is being sent. For example, UDP will be lost, but TCP will request lost segments to be resent.
For inbound traffic, it is unlikely that any links are oversubscribed, and you usually have much more inbound than outbound traffic. You really don't have much control over the traffic from the Internet to your network. If a single request from a computer on your network starts another computer on the Internet sending 1 Gbps of traffic to your network, it can basically monopolize your inbound ISP connection. Someone wanting to disrupt you network can try to overload your incoming network connection in a DoS (Denial of Service) attack.
There is QoS to try to introduce your vision of fairness to your network. For example, you can prioritize real-time traffic, e.g. VoIP. You can't really do a lot about the traffic coming into your network from the Internet because you don't control the Internet.

text:
Q: Bitrates supported by switches, routers and network cards are often 10 MBit/s, 100 MBit/s or 1GBit/s. Newer developments promise up to 10 GBit/s. I do not see a reason why powers of 10 should have a certain meaning here, unlike powers of 2 in memory storage increase.
Why do bitrates increase in that pattern?

[Score:1] You are incorrect; bitrates don't always increase by 10 times.
Memory storage increases by powers of two because it is addressed with binary (base 2) addresses.
Increasing ethernet speeds by a power of 10 has been a convenient goal, but there is now 40 Gb ethernet and 100 Gb ethernet. Also, Wi-Fi speeds have not been anything like a linear 10 times increase: 802.11
was 2 Mb, 802.11a
was 54 Mb, 802.11b
was 11 Mb, 802.11g
was 54 Mb, 802.11n
was 150 Mb per stream, etc.

text:
Q: Digital signal consists of many sine waves. Now the signal with waves of frequencies 1Mhz to 5Mhz has bandwidth of 5-1 = 4Mhz. Now the fundamental period of this signal is decided by lowest frequency that is 1Mhz. So one cycle sends 2 bits let's say, therefore 2 bits are transmitted in 1/1Mhz = 1 μs. Hence 1 bit in 0.5μs. Hence bitrate is 2Mbps.
If I increase bandwidth, from 4 to 8Mhz, for the same, does the spectrum still vary from 1Mhz to 1 + 8 = 9Mhz ? because this way by doubling bandwidth and using same lowest frequency, data rate will be same. Unless i increase bandwidth as well as increase minimum frequency. also by doubling bandwidth is it that the range also shifts towards right? eg for bandwidth 4mhz, spectrum was 1 to 5, for 8mhz bandwidth spectrum becomes 2 to 10 or how does it work?
Please explain. I'm a beginner in this and currently going through William stalling's data and computer communication book which states bandwidth increases with data rate and vice-versa where as I feel it's just lowest frequency of bandwidth thats increasing the bitrate or data rate.

[Score:1] Bandwidth increases as a result of using higher modulation frequencies, not the other way around. Bandwidth is a function of the modulation frequency. So if that is constant, so is your bandwidth.

text:
Setup TLS ConnectionÂ¶
If you want to use secure channel to connect OpenFlow switches, you need to use TLS connection. This document describes how to setup Ryu to connect to the Open vSwitch over TLS.
Configuring a Public Key InfrastructureÂ¶
If you don't have a PKI, the ovs-pki script included with Open vSwitch can help you. This section is based on the INSTALL.SSL in the Open vSwitch source code.
NOTE: How to install Open vSwitch isn't described in this document. Please refer to the Open vSwitch documents.
Create a PKI by using ovs-pki script:
% ovs-pki init
(Default directory is /usr/local/var/lib/openvswitch/pki)
The pki directory consists of controllerca and switchca subdirectories. Each directory contains CA files.
Create a controller private key and certificate:
% ovs-pki req+sign ctl controller
ctl-privkey.pem and ctl-cert.pem are generated in the current directory.
Create a switch private key and certificate:
% ovs-pki req+sign sc switch
sc-privkey.pem and sc-cert.pem are generated in the current directory.
Testing TLS ConnectionÂ¶
Configuring ovs-vswitchd to use CA files using the ovs-vsctl "set-ssl" command, e.g.:
% ovs-vsctl set-ssl /etc/openvswitch/sc-privkey.pem \
/etc/openvswitch/sc-cert.pem \
/usr/local/var/lib/openvswitch/pki/controllerca/cacert.pem
% ovs-vsctl add-br br0
% ovs-vsctl set-controller br0 ssl:127.0.0.1:6633
Substitute the correct file names, if they differ from the ones used above. You should use absolute file names.
Run Ryu with CA files:
% ryu-manager --ctl-privkey ctl-privkey.pem \
--ctl-cert ctl-cert.pem \
--ca-certs /usr/local/var/lib/openvswitch/pki/switchca/cacert.pem \
--verbose
You can see something like:
loading app ryu.controller.ofp_handler
instantiating app ryu.controller.ofp_handler

CONSUMES EventOFPErrorMsg

code_snippets.code: NOTE: How to install Open vSwitch isn't described in this document. Please refer to the Open vSwitch documents.
code_snippets.context_before:
If you want to use secure channel to connect OpenFlow switches, you need to use TLS connection. This document describes how to setup Ryu to connect to the Open vSwitch over TLS.
Configuring a Public Key InfrastructureÂ¶
If you don't have a PKI, the ovs-pki script included with Open vSwitch can help you. This section is based on the INSTALL.SSL in the Open vSwitch source code.
code_snippets.context_after:
Create a PKI by using ovs-pki script:
% ovs-pki init
(Default directory is /usr/local/var/lib/openvswitch/pki)
code_snippets.full_context:
If you want to use secure channel to connect OpenFlow switches, you need to use TLS connection. This document describes how to setup Ryu to connect to the Open vSwitch over TLS.
Configuring a Public Key InfrastructureÂ¶
If you don't have a PKI, the ovs-pki script included with Open vSwitch can help you. This section is based on the INSTALL.SSL in the Open vSwitch source code.

NOTE: How to install Open vSwitch isn't described in this document. Please refer to the Open vSwitch documents.

Create a PKI by using ovs-pki script:
% ovs-pki init
(Default directory is /usr/local/var/lib/openvswitch/pki)

text:
Ryu API ReferenceÂ¶
-
class
ryu.base.app_manager.
RyuApp
(*_args, **_kwargs)Â¶ The base class for Ryu applications.
RyuApp subclasses are instantiated after ryu-manager loaded all requested Ryu application modules. __init__ should call RyuApp.__init__ with the same arguments. It's illegal to send any events in __init__.
The instance attribute 'name' is the name of the class used for message routing among Ryu applications. (Cf. send_event) It's set to __class__.__name__ by RyuApp.__init__. It's discouraged for subclasses to override this.
-
OFP_VERSIONS
= NoneÂ¶ A list of supported OpenFlow versions for this RyuApp. The default is all versions supported by the framework.
Examples:
OFP_VERSIONS = [ofproto_v1_0.OFP_VERSION, ofproto_v1_2.OFP_VERSION]
If multiple Ryu applications are loaded in the system, the intersection of their OFP_VERSIONS is used.
-
_CONTEXTS
= {}Â¶ A dictionary to specify contexts which this Ryu application wants to use. Its key is a name of context and its value is an ordinary class which implements the context. The class is instantiated by app_manager and the instance is shared among RyuApp subclasses which has _CONTEXTS member with the same key. A RyuApp subclass can obtain a reference to the instance via its __init__'s kwargs as the following.
Example:
_CONTEXTS = { 'network': network.Network } def __init__(self, *args, *kwargs): self.network = kwargs['network']
-
_EVENTS
= []Â¶ A list of event classes which this RyuApp subclass would generate. This should be specified if and only if event classes are defined in a different python module from the RyuApp subclass is.
-
close
()Â¶ teardown method. The method name, close, is chosen for python context manager
-
classmethod
context_iteritems
()Â¶ Return iterator over the (key, contxt class) of application context
-
reply_to_request
(req, rep)Â¶ Send a reply for a synchronous request sent by send_request. The first argument should be an instance of EventRequestBase. The second argument should be an instance of EventReplyBase.
-
send_event
(name, ev, state=None)Â¶ Send the specified event to the RyuApp instance specified by name.
-
send_event_to_observers
(ev, state=None)Â¶ Send the specified event to all observers of this RyuApp.
-
send_request
(req)Â¶ Make a synchronous request. Set req.sync to True, send it to a Ryu application specified by req.dst, and block until receiving a reply. Returns the received reply. The argument should be an instance of EventRequestBase.
-
start
()Â¶ Hook that is called after startup initialization is done.
-
-
class
ryu.controller.dpset.
DPSet
(*args, **kwargs)Â¶ DPSet application manages a set of switches (datapaths) connected to this controller.
Usage Example:
# ...(snip)... from ryu.controller import dpset class MyApp(app_manager.RyuApp): _CONTEXTS = { 'dpset': dpset.DPSet, } def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs): super(MyApp, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs) # Stores DPSet instance to call its API in this app self.dpset = kwargs['dpset'] def _my_handler(self): # Get the datapath object which has the given dpid dpid = 1 dp = self.dpset.get(dpid) if dp is None: self.logger.info('No such datapath: dpid=%d', dpid)
-
get
(dp_id)Â¶ This method returns the ryu.controller.controller.Datapath instance for the given Datapath ID.
-
get_all
()Â¶ This method returns a list of tuples which represents instances for switches connected to this controller. The tuple consists of a Datapath ID and an instance of ryu.controller.controller.Datapath.
A return value looks like the following:
[ (dpid_A, Datapath_A), (dpid_B, Datapath_B), ... ]
-
get_port
(dpid, port_no)Â¶ This method returns the ryu.controller.dpset.PortState instance for the given Datapath ID and the port number. Raises ryu_exc.PortNotFound if no such a datapath connected to this controller or no such a port exists.
-
get_ports
(dpid)Â¶ This method returns a list of ryu.controller.dpset.PortState instances for the given Datapath ID. Raises KeyError if no such a datapath connected to this controller.
-

text:
Ryu application APIÂ¶
Ryu application programming modelÂ¶
Threads, events, and event queuesÂ¶
Ryu applications are single-threaded entities which implement various functionalities in Ryu. Events are messages between them.
Ryu applications send asynchronous events to each other. Besides that, there are some Ryu-internal event sources which are not Ryu applications. One of examples of such event sources is OpenFlow controller. While an event can currently contain arbitrary python objects, it's discouraged to pass complex objects (eg. unpickleable objects) between Ryu applications.
Each Ryu application has a receive queue for events. The queue is FIFO and preserves the order of events. Each Ryu application has a thread for event processing. The thread keeps draining the receive queue by dequeueing an event and calling the appropritate event handler for the event type. Because the event handler is called in the context of the event processing thread, it should be careful when blocking. While an event handler is blocked, no further events for the Ryu application will be processed.
There are kinds of events which are used to implement synchronous inter-application calls between Ryu applications. While such requests uses the same machinary as ordinary events, their replies are put on a queue dedicated to the transaction to avoid deadlock.
While threads and queues is currently implemented with eventlet/greenlet, a direct use of them in a Ryu application is strongly discouraged.
Create a Ryu applicationÂ¶
A Ryu application is a python module which defines a subclass of ryu.base.app_manager.RyuApp. If two or more such classes are defined in a module, the first one (by name order) will be picked by app_manager. Ryu application is singleton: only single instance of a given Ryu application is supported.
Observe eventsÂ¶
A Ryu application can register itself to listen for specific events using ryu.controller.handler.set_ev_cls decorator.
Generate eventsÂ¶
A Ryu application can raise events by calling appropriate ryu.base.app_manager.RyuApp's methods like send_event or send_event_to_observers.
Event classesÂ¶
An event class describes a Ryu event generated in the system. By convention, event class names are prefixed by "Event". Events are generated either by the core part of Ryu or Ryu applications. A Ryu application can register its interest for a specific type of event by providing a handler method using ryu.controller.handler.set_ev_cls decorator.
OpenFlow event classesÂ¶
ryu.controller.ofp_event module exports event classes which describe receptions of OpenFlow messages from connected switches. By convention, they are named as ryu.controller.ofp_event.EventOFPxxxx where xxxx is the name of the corresponding OpenFlow message. For example, EventOFPPacketIn for packet-in message. The OpenFlow controller part of Ryu automatically decodes OpenFlow messages received from switches and send these events to Ryu applications which expressed an interest using ryu.controller.handler.set_ev_cls. OpenFlow event classes are subclasses of the following class.
-
class
ryu.controller.ofp_event.
EventOFPMsgBase
(msg)Â¶ The base class of OpenFlow event class.
OpenFlow event classes have at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description msg An object which describes the corresponding OpenFlow message. msg.datapath A ryu.controller.controller.Datapath instance which describes an OpenFlow switch from which we received this OpenFlow message. timestamp Timestamp when Datapath instance generated this event. The msg object has some more additional members whose values are extracted from the original OpenFlow message.
See OpenFlow protocol API Reference for more info about OpenFlow messages.
ryu.controller.handler.set_ev_clsÂ¶
-
ryu.controller.handler.
set_ev_cls
(ev_cls, dispatchers=None)Â¶ A decorator for Ryu application to declare an event handler.
Decorated method will become an event handler. ev_cls is an event class whose instances this RyuApp wants to receive. dispatchers argument specifies one of the following negotiation phases (or a list of them) for which events should be generated for this handler. Note that, in case an event changes the phase, the phase before the change is used to check the interest.
Negotiation phase Description ryu.controller.handler.HANDSHAKE_DISPATCHER Sending and waiting for hello message ryu.controller.handler.CONFIG_DISPATCHER Version negotiated and sent features-request message ryu.controller.handler.MAIN_DISPATCHER Switch-features message received and sent set-config message ryu.controller.handler.DEAD_DISPATCHER Disconnect from the peer. Or disconnecting due to some unrecoverable errors.
ryu.controller.controller.DatapathÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.controller.
Datapath
(socket, address)Â¶ A class to describe an OpenFlow switch connected to this controller.
An instance has the following attributes.
Attribute Description id 64-bit OpenFlow Datapath ID. Only available for ryu.controller.handler.MAIN_DISPATCHER phase. ofproto A module which exports OpenFlow definitions, mainly constants appeared in the specification, for the negotiated OpenFlow version. For example, ryu.ofproto.ofproto_v1_0 for OpenFlow 1.0. ofproto_parser A module which exports OpenFlow wire message encoder and decoder for the negotiated OpenFlow version. For example, ryu.ofproto.ofproto_v1_0_parser for OpenFlow 1.0. ofproto_parser.OFPxxxx(datapath,...) A callable to prepare an OpenFlow message for the given switch. It can be sent with Datapath.send_msg later. xxxx is a name of the message. For example OFPFlowMod for flow-mod message. Arguemnts depend on the message. set_xid(self, msg) Generate an OpenFlow XID and put it in msg.xid. send_msg(self, msg) Queue an OpenFlow message to send to the corresponding switch. If msg.xid is None, set_xid is automatically called on the message before queueing. send_packet_out deprecated send_flow_mod deprecated send_flow_del deprecated send_delete_all_flows deprecated send_barrier Queue an OpenFlow barrier message to send to the switch. send_nxt_set_flow_format deprecated is_reserved_port deprecated
ryu.controller.event.EventBaseÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.event.
EventBase
Â¶ The base of all event classes.
A Ryu application can define its own event type by creating a subclass.
ryu.controller.event.EventRequestBaseÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.event.
EventRequestBase
Â¶ The base class for synchronous request for RyuApp.send_request.
ryu.controller.event.EventReplyBaseÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.event.
EventReplyBase
(dst)Â¶ The base class for synchronous request reply for RyuApp.send_reply.
ryu.controller.ofp_event.EventOFPStateChangeÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.ofp_event.
EventOFPStateChange
(dp)Â¶ An event class for negotiation phase change notification.
An instance of this class is sent to observer after changing the negotiation phase. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description datapath ryu.controller.controller.Datapath instance of the switch
ryu.controller.ofp_event.EventOFPPortStateChangeÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.ofp_event.
EventOFPPortStateChange
(dp, reason, port_no)Â¶ An event class to notify the port state changes of Dtatapath instance.
This event performs like EventOFPPortStatus, but Ryu will send this event after updating
ports
dict of Datapath instances. An instance has at least the following attributes.Attribute Description datapath ryu.controller.controller.Datapath instance of the switch reason one of OFPPR_* port_no Port number which state was changed
ryu.controller.dpset.EventDPÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.dpset.
EventDP
(dp, enter_leave)Â¶ An event class to notify connect/disconnect of a switch.
For OpenFlow switches, one can get the same notification by observing ryu.controller.ofp_event.EventOFPStateChange. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description dp A ryu.controller.controller.Datapath instance of the switch enter True when the switch connected to our controller. False for disconnect. ports A list of port instances.
ryu.controller.dpset.EventPortAddÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.dpset.
EventPortAdd
(dp, port)Â¶ An event class for switch port status "ADD" notification.
This event is generated when a new port is added to a switch. For OpenFlow switches, one can get the same notification by observing ryu.controller.ofp_event.EventOFPPortStatus. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description dp A ryu.controller.controller.Datapath instance of the switch port port number
ryu.controller.dpset.EventPortDeleteÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.dpset.
EventPortDelete
(dp, port)Â¶ An event class for switch port status "DELETE" notification.
This event is generated when a port is removed from a switch. For OpenFlow switches, one can get the same notification by observing ryu.controller.ofp_event.EventOFPPortStatus. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description dp A ryu.controller.controller.Datapath instance of the switch port port number
ryu.controller.dpset.EventPortModifyÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.dpset.
EventPortModify
(dp, new_port)Â¶ An event class for switch port status "MODIFY" notification.
This event is generated when some attribute of a port is changed. For OpenFlow switches, one can get the same notification by observing ryu.controller.ofp_event.EventOFPPortStatus. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description dp A ryu.controller.controller.Datapath instance of the switch port port number
ryu.controller.network.EventNetworkPortÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.network.
EventNetworkPort
(network_id, dpid, port_no, add_del)Â¶ An event class for notification of port arrival and deperture.
This event is generated when a port is introduced to or removed from a network by the REST API. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description network_id Network ID dpid OpenFlow Datapath ID of the switch to which the port belongs. port_no OpenFlow port number of the port add_del True for adding a port. False for removing a port.
ryu.controller.network.EventNetworkDelÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.network.
EventNetworkDel
(network_id)Â¶ An event class for network deletion.
This event is generated when a network is deleted by the REST API. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description network_id Network ID
ryu.controller.network.EventMacAddressÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.network.
EventMacAddress
(dpid, port_no, network_id, mac_address, add_del)Â¶ An event class for end-point MAC address registration.
This event is generated when a end-point MAC address is updated by the REST API. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description network_id Network ID dpid OpenFlow Datapath ID of the switch to which the port belongs. port_no OpenFlow port number of the port mac_address The old MAC address of the port if add_del is False. Otherwise the new MAC address. add_del False if this event is a result of a port removal. Otherwise True.
ryu.controller.tunnels.EventTunnelKeyAddÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.tunnels.
EventTunnelKeyAdd
(network_id, tunnel_key)Â¶ An event class for tunnel key registration.
This event is generated when a tunnel key is registered or updated by the REST API. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description network_id Network ID tunnel_key Tunnel Key
ryu.controller.tunnels.EventTunnelKeyDelÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.tunnels.
EventTunnelKeyDel
(network_id, tunnel_key)Â¶ An event class for tunnel key registration.
This event is generated when a tunnel key is removed by the REST API. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description network_id Network ID tunnel_key Tunnel Key
ryu.controller.tunnels.EventTunnelPortÂ¶
-
class
ryu.controller.tunnels.
EventTunnelPort
(dpid, port_no, remote_dpid, add_del)Â¶ An event class for tunnel port registration.
This event is generated when a tunnel port is added or removed by the REST API. An instance has at least the following attributes.
Attribute Description dpid OpenFlow Datapath ID port_no OpenFlow port number remote_dpid OpenFlow port number of the tunnel peer add_del True for adding a tunnel. False for removal.

Event classesÂ¶
An event class describes a Ryu event generated in the system. By convention, event class names are prefixed by "Event". Events are generated either by the core part of Ryu or Ryu applications. A Ryu application can register its interest for a specific type of event by providing a handler method using ryu.controller.handler.set_ev_cls decorator.
OpenFlow event classesÂ¶

ryu.controller.ofp_event module exports event classes which describe receptions of OpenFlow messages from connected switches. By convention, they are named as ryu.controller.ofp_event.EventOFPxxxx where xxxx is the name of the corresponding OpenFlow message. For example, EventOFPPacketIn for packet-in message. The OpenFlow controller part of Ryu automatically decodes OpenFlow messages received from switches and send these events to Ryu applications which expressed an interest using ryu.controller.handler.set_ev_cls. OpenFlow event classes are subclasses of the following class.

-
class
ryu.controller.ofp_event.

---

Datapath
(socket, address)Â¶ A class to describe an OpenFlow switch connected to this controller.
An instance has the following attributes.

Attribute Description id 64-bit OpenFlow Datapath ID. Only available for ryu.controller.handler.MAIN_DISPATCHER phase. ofproto A module which exports OpenFlow definitions, mainly constants appeared in the specification, for the negotiated OpenFlow version. For example, ryu.ofproto.ofproto_v1_0 for OpenFlow 1.0. ofproto_parser A module which exports OpenFlow wire message encoder and decoder for the negotiated OpenFlow version. For example, ryu.ofproto.ofproto_v1_0_parser for OpenFlow 1.0. ofproto_parser.OFPxxxx(datapath,...) A callable to prepare an OpenFlow message for the given switch. It can be sent with Datapath.send_msg later. xxxx is a name of the message. For example OFPFlowMod for flow-mod message. Arguemnts depend on the message. set_xid(self, msg) Generate an OpenFlow XID and put it in msg.xid. send_msg(self, msg) Queue an OpenFlow message to send to the corresponding switch. If msg.xid is None, set_xid is automatically called on the message before queueing. send_packet_out deprecated send_flow_mod deprecated send_flow_del deprecated send_delete_all_flows deprecated send_barrier Queue an OpenFlow barrier message to send to the switch. send_nxt_set_flow_format deprecated is_reserved_port deprecated

text: The text references Python 2.5 metaclass directives ('new-style class') which are obsolete. Modern Mininet (Python 3) handles logging differently without these specific base class constraints.
