text:
|
Mininet Python API Reference Manual
|
| ▼Nmininet | |
| ▼Nclean | |
| CCleanup | Wrapper for cleanup() |
| ▼Ncli | |
| CCLI | Simple command-line interface to talk to nodes |
| ▼Nlink | |
| CIntf | Basic interface object that can configure itself |
| CLink | A basic link is just a veth pair |
| COVSIntf | Patch interface on an OVSSwitch |
| COVSLink | Link that makes patch links between OVSSwitches |
| CTCIntf | Interface customized by tc (traffic control) utility Allows specification of bandwidth limits (various methods) as well as delay, loss and max queue length |
| CTCLink | Link with TC interfaces |

metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Comprehensive and accurate Mininet class reference; code snippets are syntactically correct.
metadata.context_summary: Documentation for the OVSLink class, which facilitates patch link creation between OVSSwitches, including specific warnings about link limits.

text: The documentation notes that `MininetWithControlNet` is rarely used and the code has been moved to its own class. In modern Mininet, explicit control networks are often unnecessary as switches/controllers can use loopback or Unix sockets by default.

text: The `CPULimitedHost` class relies on Linux cgroups. For accurate operation, the host kernel must have `CONFIG_CGROUP_CPU_ACCT` and `CONFIG_CGROUP_CPUSET` enabled, and the user may need to manually create cgroups if the default ones are insufficient.

metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Accurate reference for SingleSwitchReversedTopo; correctly describes the port reversal logic.
metadata.context_summary: API reference for the MininetWithControlNet class, focusing on the configuration of explicit routed control networks for controllers and switches.

text: While the base `Topo` class is generic, the title suggests a specific data center topology. The `Topo` class itself is a generic graph representation used by all topologies (e.g., `TreeTopo`, `LinearTopo`), not just data center ones.

metadata.title: Mininet Python API Reference Manual: mininet.link.TCULink Class Reference
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Accurate Topo class reference, but the title 'Data center network representation' is misleading for the base `Topo` class.
metadata.context_summary: API reference for the Node class, describing virtual network nodes as shells in network namespaces with methods for command execution and interface management.

text: The `TreeTopo` class creates a binary tree by default (fanout=2) unless specified otherwise. Users should be aware that the switch port count must accommodate the `fanout` parameter; otherwise, the topology build may fail if switches run out of ports.

Control network.
| support | Create an explicit control network. Currently this is only used/usable with the user datapath. @param Notes 1. If the controller and switches are in the same (e.g. root) namespace, they can just use the loopback connection. 2. If we can get unix domain sockets to work, we can use them instead of an explicit control network. 3. Instead of routing, we could bridge or use 'in-band' control. 4. Even if we dispense with this in general, it could still be useful for people who wish to simulate a separate control network (since real networks may need one!) 5. Basically nobody ever used this code, so it has been moved into its own class. 6. Ultimately we may wish to extend this to allow us to create a control network which every node's control interface is attached to. |

metadata.title: Mininet Python API Reference Manual: mininet.net.MininetWithControlNet Class Reference
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Accurate introduction to OpenDaylight and its microservices architecture.
metadata.context_summary: This document provides a technical reference for the `MininetWithControlNet` class within the Mininet Python API, detailing its specific methods and attributes for programmatically managing network topologies. It serves as a guide for developers implementing control network functionalities using Mininet's simulation framework.

code_snippets.context_before:
Control network.
| support | Create an explicit control network. Currently this is only used/usable with the user datapath. @param Notes 1. If the controller and switches are in the same (e.g. root) namespace, they can just use the loopback connection. 2. If we can get unix domain sockets to work, we can use them instead of an explicit control network. 3. Instead of routing, we could bridge or use 'in-band' control. 4. Even if we dispense with this in general, it could still be useful for people who wish to simulate a separate control network (since real networks may need one!) 5. Basically nobody ever used this code, so it has been moved into its own class. 6. Ultimately we may wish to extend this to allow us to create a control network which every node's control interface is attached to. |

metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: Comprehensive and accurate security guide for OpenDaylight; includes specific mitigation steps.
metadata.context_summary: This document provides a theoretical reference for the `Node` class within the Mininet Python API, detailing its attributes and methods for managing virtual network nodes. It serves as a guide for developers to instantiate and configure nodes in software-defined networking simulations.

metadata.title: Mininet Python API Reference Manual: mininet.node.CPULimitedHost Class Reference
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The document provides accurate, official guidance on OpenDaylight documentation standards, tooling (reStructuredText/Sphinx), and specific version-agnostic best practices for contribution and formatting.
metadata.context_summary: This document provides a technical reference for the `CPULimitedHost` class within the Mininet Python API, detailing its role in simulating hosts with constrained CPU resources for network emulation. It covers class attributes, methods, and initialization parameters necessary for developers implementing CPU-limited virtual machines in Mininet topologies.

text: Mininet is a network emulator that creates virtual network topologies using real Linux namespaces, veth pairs, and Linux bridges. It requires Python 3 (Python 2 is deprecated) and typically needs a controller like Ryu, POX, or ONOS to function. The switches in Mininet are software-based (Linux bridges) emulating OpenFlow behavior, not physical hardware. Installation is most commonly done via `pip install mininet` or cloning the repository, rather than the outdated 'virtual machine' or 'package manager' methods mentioned. The CLI commands listed are basic; advanced usage relies heavily on Python scripting to define topologies (e.g., `net = sdn.network('tree', 3)`).

metadata.title: Mininet Python API Reference Manual: mininet.topo.MinimalTopo Class Reference
metadata.llm_evaluation_reason: The content is technically vague and lacks depth; it omits critical details like the requirement for Python 3, the specific role of Ryu or POX controllers, and the distinction between Mininet's virtual switches and real OpenFlow switches. The installation methods are also outdated.
metadata.context_summary: This document provides a technical reference for the `MinimalTopo` class within the Mininet Python API, detailing its role in defining minimal network topologies for simulation. It serves as a theoretical guide for developers looking to implement or extend basic network structures using this specific class.
